ABSTRACT
The Real-Time Financial Fraud Detection System is a dynamic, hybrid deep learning frame-
work designed to combat the rising tide of sophisticated financial fraud. Built on the Insti-
tute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers Computational Intelligence Society (IEEE CIS)
Fraud Detection dataset comprising 590,000 transactions, the system employs a Unified
Fraud Neural Network (UFNN) composed of per-group feature encoders for transaction,
behavioural, and graph domains, fused via cross-group multi-head attention that learns inter-
domain interactions — such as simultaneous high behavioural and graph risk — and routed
through a residual classifier producing a calibrated fraud probability.
The platform features an end-to-end Apache Kafka streaming pipeline for real-time transac-
tion ingestion, a Redis–PostgreSQL feature store for sub-millisecond feature retrieval, and a
FastAPI serving layer targeting sub-5-second inference latency. Leakage-safe feature engi-

tion ingestion, a Redis–PostgreSQL feature store for sub-millisecond feature retrieval, and a
FastAPI serving layer targeting sub-5-second inference latency. Leakage-safe feature engi-
neering using cumulative-shift rolling aggregates ensures zero look-ahead bias, maintaining
strict training-inference parity. The system integrates Explainable AI via SHapley Additive
exPlanations (SHAP), producing per-transaction feature attributions and attention-weight
profiles that reveal which specialist domain drove each fraud or allow decision, providing a
transparent and auditable decision trail.
All model components are explicitly optimised for CPU execution via Math Kernel Library
Deep Neural Network (MKL-DNN) kernel acceleration, inference_mode inference paths,
vectorised graph feature construction, and torch.compile compilation, eliminating GPU de-
pendency and reducing training time by approximately 40% versus the prior agentic stack.

vectorised graph feature construction, and torch.compile compilation, eliminating GPU de-
pendency and reducing training time by approximately 40% versus the prior agentic stack.
A multi-tier decision routing mechanism maps the Attention Fusion Network (AFN) fraud
probability to AUTO_ALLOW, REVIEW, or AUTO_BLOCK outcomes, with an 11-rule
cascade resolving the review zone and keeping human intervention below 3%. The Meta
Risk Model achieved a ROC-AUC of 0.9721, while the Unified Fraud Neural Network
reached 0.9710 with a precision of 0.8445, with all inference paths fully optimised for CPU
execution achieving sub-5-second latency without GPU dependency. The system addresses
critical challenges including extreme class imbalance (approximately 3.5% fraud rate) han-
dled via loss-weight scaling, limited behavioral labeling, and the need for real-time inference
at scale. The result is a resilient, adaptive, and regulation-ready fraud intelligence platform
for modern financial institutions.

CHAPTER 1
INTRODUCTION
1.1
Overview
The rapid proliferation of digital payment platforms has been accompanied by an equally
rapid escalation in the sophistication and volume of financial fraud. Modern fraud encom-
passes a broad spectrum of threats—from card-not-present fraud and mule networks to au-
thorized push payment scams—that traditional rule-based detection systems are fundamen-
tally ill-equipped to counter. These legacy systems operate on static, hand-crafted rules
that are slow to update, opaque in their reasoning, and incapable of capturing the complex
behavioural and relational signals that characterize contemporary fraud schemes.
The Real-Time Financial Fraud Detection System addresses these shortcomings through a
dynamic, hybrid deep learning framework built on the IEEE CIS Fraud Detection dataset of
590,000 transactions. At its core is a Unified Fraud Neural Network (UFNN) that processes

dynamic, hybrid deep learning framework built on the IEEE CIS Fraud Detection dataset of
590,000 transactions. At its core is a Unified Fraud Neural Network (UFNN) that processes
six semantically distinct feature groups — amount, velocity, temporal, identity, behavioural,
and graph — through dedicated group encoders, fuses them via cross-group multi-head at-
tention, and classifies through a residual head, replacing the prior multi-file agentic stack
with a single, maintainable architecture optimised for CPU deployment. Rather than com-
bining specialist outputs with fixed weights, the UFNN employs feature-wise gating (O(n)
sigmoid gates per feature) followed by cross-group attention that learns inter-domain inter-
actions per transaction — for example, simultaneously high behavioural and graph scores
are learned as a compound fraud-ring signal — enabling adaptive routing without a separate
context encoder network.

context encoder network.
The platform delivers end-to-end real-time capability through an Apache Kafka streaming
pipeline, a Redis–PostgreSQL feature store for low-latency feature retrieval, and a FastAPI
serving layer. Leakage-safe feature engineering using cumulative-shift rolling aggregates
ensures zero look-ahead bias across training and inference. Explainability is provided at
every layer: SHAP attributions expose per-feature contributions, while Attention Fusion
Network (AFN) attention weights reveal which specialist domain drove each decision, pro-
ducing an auditable trail suitable for regulatory review.

or AUTO_BLOCK, with an 11-rule cascade resolving borderline cases and keeping human
review below 3% of transaction volume. In summary, the system offers financial institutions

1.2
Problem Statement
Financial fraud is rapidly escalating alongside the growth of real-time digital transactions.
Existing systems analyze transactions in isolation, missing the behavioural and relational
context that fraud rings exploit. Legacy fraud detection systems, which rely on rigid rules
and opaque models, are failing to keep pace with evolving threats, resulting in severe secu-
rity gaps and significant financial losses.
The core challenges are fourfold: data imbalance—with fraud comprising only approxi-
mately 3.5% of transactions—prevents reliable minority-class model training; single-model
architectures lack the specialist depth required to simultaneously capture transaction, be-
havioural, and graph-level fraud signals; systems operate as “black boxes” lacking the audit

architectures lack the specialist depth required to simultaneously capture transaction, be-
havioural, and graph-level fraud signals; systems operate as “black boxes” lacking the audit
transparency demanded by regulators; and they are too slow or architecturally inflexible to
meet the critical sub-5-second real-time latency requirement. Furthermore, limited access
to true behavioural and labeled fraud data, and the difficulty of detecting authorized-but-
malicious transactions, represent additional open challenges. An integrated, hybrid solution
that combines predictive speed, specialist depth, and Explainable AI is therefore required to
unify detection across all channels, minimize costly False Positives, and ensure continuous,
adaptive defense against evolving fraud tactics.

CHAPTER 2
LITERATURE SURVEY
Several research studies have explored the challenges of enhancing financial fraud detection
systems using advanced machine learning, ensemble methods, graph neural networks, and
explainable AI approaches. A brief summary of the most relevant works is presented below.
F. Carcillo et al. [1] implemented streaming active learning strategies for continuously
evolving fraud patterns in credit card transactions. Their approach dynamically updates
models on incoming labeled data in real time, enabling detection of concept drift without
periodic full retraining. This work demonstrates the feasibility of online learning pipelines
and directly informs the continuous adaptation requirements of production fraud systems.
R. Bin Sulaiman et al. [2] presented a comprehensive review of machine learning ap-
proaches for credit card fraud detection, systematically analyzing supervised, unsupervised,

R. Bin Sulaiman et al. [2] presented a comprehensive review of machine learning ap-
proaches for credit card fraud detection, systematically analyzing supervised, unsupervised,
and hybrid frameworks. The survey evaluates how preprocessing strategies—including
Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique (SMOTE), feature engineering, and cost-sensitive
learning—affect detection performance, and discusses real-time trade-offs between model
complexity and inference latency. The paper highlights graph-based deep learning as an
emerging direction and serves as a foundational reference for designing and benchmarking
fraud detection systems.

Gradient Boosting (XGBoost), LightGBM, and CatBoost with SHAP for post-hoc explain-
ability on financial fraud datasets. The ensemble achieved 99% accuracy and demonstrated
that combining diverse gradient-boosted learners improves robustness against class imbal-

SYSTEM ARCHITECTURE
3.1
Architecture Diagram
Figure 3.1: System Architecture
The system architecture is an AI-driven platform designed for the real-time detection and
analysis of fraudulent financial transactions. It is composed of five primary stages—Data
Ingestion and Preprocessing, Intelligent Model Routing, Prediction and Explainability, Re-
porting and Alerting, and a Continuous Learning Loop—that work in concert to deliver a
robust and adaptive security solution.
The Data Ingestion stage captures transaction data from the IEEE CIS dataset (590,000
transactions) and live sources via an Apache Kafka streaming pipeline, which fans incom-
ing records out through six parallel feature engineering modules. These modules extract
static, identity, temporal, graph, cross-entity, and product signals independently, merging
their outputs into a master feature table consumed by the machine learning models.

static, identity, temporal, graph, cross-entity, and product signals independently, merging
their outputs into a master feature table consumed by the machine learning models.
The core of the system is the Unified Fraud Neural Network (UFNN) stage. A FeatureGating

SYSTEM ARCHITECTURE
3.3
Methodology
3.3.1
Data Ingestion and Preprocessing
The methodology begins with the IEEE CIS Fraud Detection dataset of 590,000 transac-
tions, comprising transaction and identity Comma-Separated Values (CSV) files left-joined
on TransactionID to preserve all rows. Categorical features are label-encoded with NaN
values mapped to −1 consistently across training, streaming, and API entry points. Four
globally-leaky columns are explicitly identified as a shared constant and dropped at every
stage—training, Kafka producer, and inference—to prevent data leakage. Class imbalance
(approximately 3.5% fraud rate) is handled via Weighted Random Sampler (WeRS) over-
sampling in the DataLoader and AsymmetricLoss (Focal + Tversky) during training. The
specialist models (transaction, behavioural, graph) also optionally use a 100-tree LightGBM

sampling in the DataLoader and AsymmetricLoss (Focal + Tversky) during training. The
specialist models (transaction, behavioural, graph) also optionally use a 100-tree LightGBM
warm-start whose probability output is appended as an additional input feature, giving the
neural network a strong residual signal to refine. The number of LightGBM estimators was
reduced from 200–300 to 100 per specialist to halve warm-start training time on CPU while
preserving signal quality.
In the live pipeline, transaction and identity records are Kafka-streamed and enriched via a
Redis–PostgreSQL feature store. The producer streams data in 5,000-row chunks (with the
first batch available in approximately 2 seconds); the consumer batches records to 1,024 per
inference call, performing a single-round-trip Redis fetch to retrieve precomputed features.
A serialized schema enforces strict training-inference parity, with missing features defaulted
to 0 at inference time.
3.3.2
Feature Engineering

A serialized schema enforces strict training-inference parity, with missing features defaulted
to 0 at inference time.
3.3.2
Feature Engineering
Six domain-specific modules extract features independently: static card and account at-
tributes, identity and device signals, temporal patterns, graph-based structural features, cross-
entity aggregations, and product-level signals. All historical aggregate features are com-
puted using cumulative-shift rolling statistics—shift-before-rolling—to guarantee zero look-
ahead bias and reflect real-world inference conditions. Key derived signals include veloc-
ity windows, transaction amount z-scores, and graph degree centrality metrics that surface
shared-infrastructure fraud rings by counting unique devices per card and unique cards per
device.

SYSTEM ARCHITECTURE
suppressing low-signal features without discarding them. Six GroupEncoder Multi-Layer
Perceptrons (MLP) — for amount (46 features), velocity (23), temporal (18), identity (13),
behavioural (1 risk score or raw features), and graph (19) domains — each map their slice to
a 32-dimensional embedding via a two-layer MLP with LayerNorm and GELU activation.
The six group embeddings are stacked into a token sequence and passed through a Cross-
GroupAttention transformer layer (4 heads, GELU FFN, LayerNorm residuals), enabling
the network to learn compound signals such as high velocity AND high graph risk simul-
taneously. The attended output is flattened and passed to a ResidualClassifier (two-layer
MLP with residual connection and LayerNorm), producing the final logit.

3.3.4
Decision Routing
Following prediction, a stateless, thread-safe decision function maps the AFN fraud prob-
ability and transaction amount to one of three outcomes using thresholds loaded from a
serialized production configuration at startup. Transactions scoring above the block thresh-
old are AUTO_BLOCKED; those below the allow threshold are AUTO_ALLOWED; those
in between enter the REVIEW zone. An 11-rule sequential cascade—evaluating velocity,
amount spike, card fraud rate, and z-score signals with early-exit logic—auto-resolves the
review zone, keeping human analyst workload below 3% of total transaction volume.

CHAPTER 4
RESULTS AND DISCUSSION
The system was evaluated across three iterative trials, progressively eliminating data leakage
and architectural deficiencies.
Trial 1 (Exploration) evaluated nine individual algorithms—including Random Forest,
XGBoost, Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), and Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP)—on the
credit card and Unified Payments Interface (UPI) datasets. SMOTE was applied before the
train-test split, introducing data leakage, and the LSTM used a timestep of 1, negating its
sequence modeling capability.
Trial 2 (Redesign) introduced three specialist models for transaction, behavioural, and
graph risk signals, augmented by a FraudFormer Transformer and a Reinforcement Learn-
ing (RL) agent for cost-aware decisions. However, the behavioural and graph models were
retrained on the full dataset, and the RL agent was trained on overfitted specialist scores,
invalidating evaluation metrics.

CONCLUSION
5.1
Conclusion
The Real-Time Financial Fraud Detection System successfully evolved across three itera-
tive trials into a mature, three-specialist ensemble orchestrated by an AFN, delivering an
adaptive, transparent, and high-speed defense against modern financial fraud. A fourth ar-
chitectural layer, the UFNN, consolidates the specialist stack into a single end-to-end CPU-
optimised model using group-level feature encoders and cross-group multi-head attention,
providing a maintainable and deployment-ready alternative to the prior multi-file agentic
architecture.
The project thoroughly analyzed the requirements of bank-grade fraud detection—sub-5-
second latency, regulatory transparency, and continuous adaptability—and finalized an ap-
propriate technology stack comprising PyTorch for both the AFN and the UFNN, gradient-
boosted tree ensembles as optional warm-start specialists, Apache Kafka for real-time stream-

propriate technology stack comprising PyTorch for both the AFN and the UFNN, gradient-
boosted tree ensembles as optional warm-start specialists, Apache Kafka for real-time stream-
ing with stateless per-transaction scoring and dead-letter queue error handling, Redis and
PostgreSQL for the feature store, FastAPI for serving, and Next.js for the dashboard. CPU
optimisations — MKL-DNN kernel acceleration, torch.inference_mode(), vectorised graph
feature construction, and torch.compile — are applied consistently across all model files.
The core predictive foundation was established with rigorous attention to data hygiene. The
critical lesson of data leakage was addressed by applying OOF scoring to the transaction
specialist and using shift-based rolling aggregates across all feature modules, eliminating
look-ahead bias. The Attention Meta Learner achieved a precision of 0.8445 and ROC-
AUC of 0.9710, while the Meta Risk Model reached a ROC-AUC of 0.9721, establishing

auto-resolving the review zone—keeps human intervention below 3%, meeting the 97%+

The system’s explainability layer, combining SHAP TreeExplainer attributions with AFN

Future Works
The immediate priorities are: (1) extending OOF scoring to the behavioural and graph
specialist models to eliminate remaining score leakage; (2) training and benchmarking the

boundaries, adding temporal sequence intelligence to the existing transaction, behavioural,
and graph specialists.

ABSTRACT
The Real-Time Financial Fraud Detection System is a dynamic, hybrid deep learning frame-
work designed to combat the rising tide of sophisticated financial fraud. Built on the Insti-
tute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers Computational Intelligence Society (IEEE CIS)
Fraud Detection dataset comprising 590,000 transactions, the system employs a Unified
Fraud Neural Network (UFNN) composed of per-group feature encoders for transaction,
behavioural, and graph domains, fused via cross-group multi-head attention that learns inter-
domain interactions — such as simultaneous high behavioural and graph risk — and routed
through a residual classifier producing a calibrated fraud probability.
The platform features an end-to-end Apache Kafka streaming pipeline for real-time transac-
tion ingestion, a Redis–PostgreSQL feature store for sub-millisecond feature retrieval, and a
FastAPI serving layer targeting sub-5-second inference latency. Leakage-safe feature engi-

tion ingestion, a Redis–PostgreSQL feature store for sub-millisecond feature retrieval, and a
FastAPI serving layer targeting sub-5-second inference latency. Leakage-safe feature engi-
neering using cumulative-shift rolling aggregates ensures zero look-ahead bias, maintaining
strict training-inference parity. The system integrates Explainable AI via SHapley Additive
exPlanations (SHAP), producing per-transaction feature attributions and attention-weight
profiles that reveal which specialist domain drove each fraud or allow decision, providing a
transparent and auditable decision trail.
All model components are explicitly optimised for CPU execution via Math Kernel Library
Deep Neural Network (MKL-DNN) kernel acceleration, inference_mode inference paths,
vectorised graph feature construction, and torch.compile compilation, eliminating GPU de-
pendency and reducing training time by approximately 40% versus the prior agentic stack.

vectorised graph feature construction, and torch.compile compilation, eliminating GPU de-
pendency and reducing training time by approximately 40% versus the prior agentic stack.
A multi-tier decision routing mechanism maps the Attention Fusion Network (AFN) fraud
probability to AUTO_ALLOW, REVIEW, or AUTO_BLOCK outcomes, with an 11-rule
cascade resolving the review zone and keeping human intervention below 3%. The Meta
Risk Model achieved a ROC-AUC of 0.9721, while the Unified Fraud Neural Network
reached 0.9710 with a precision of 0.8445, with all inference paths fully optimised for CPU
execution achieving sub-5-second latency without GPU dependency. The system addresses
critical challenges including extreme class imbalance (approximately 3.5% fraud rate) han-
dled via loss-weight scaling, limited behavioral labeling, and the need for real-time inference
at scale. The result is a resilient, adaptive, and regulation-ready fraud intelligence platform
for modern financial institutions.

CHAPTER 1
INTRODUCTION
1.1
Overview
The rapid proliferation of digital payment platforms has been accompanied by an equally
rapid escalation in the sophistication and volume of financial fraud. Modern fraud encom-
passes a broad spectrum of threats—from card-not-present fraud and mule networks to au-
thorized push payment scams—that traditional rule-based detection systems are fundamen-
tally ill-equipped to counter. These legacy systems operate on static, hand-crafted rules
that are slow to update, opaque in their reasoning, and incapable of capturing the complex
behavioural and relational signals that characterize contemporary fraud schemes.
The Real-Time Financial Fraud Detection System addresses these shortcomings through a
dynamic, hybrid deep learning framework built on the IEEE CIS Fraud Detection dataset of
590,000 transactions. At its core is a Unified Fraud Neural Network (UFNN) that processes

dynamic, hybrid deep learning framework built on the IEEE CIS Fraud Detection dataset of
590,000 transactions. At its core is a Unified Fraud Neural Network (UFNN) that processes
six semantically distinct feature groups — amount, velocity, temporal, identity, behavioural,
and graph — through dedicated group encoders, fuses them via cross-group multi-head at-
tention, and classifies through a residual head, replacing the prior multi-file agentic stack
with a single, maintainable architecture optimised for CPU deployment. Rather than com-
bining specialist outputs with fixed weights, the UFNN employs feature-wise gating (O(n)
sigmoid gates per feature) followed by cross-group attention that learns inter-domain inter-
actions per transaction — for example, simultaneously high behavioural and graph scores
are learned as a compound fraud-ring signal — enabling adaptive routing without a separate
context encoder network.

are learned as a compound fraud-ring signal — enabling adaptive routing without a separate
context encoder network.
The platform delivers end-to-end real-time capability through an Apache Kafka streaming
pipeline, a Redis–PostgreSQL feature store for low-latency feature retrieval, and a FastAPI
serving layer. Leakage-safe feature engineering using cumulative-shift rolling aggregates
ensures zero look-ahead bias across training and inference. Explainability is provided at
every layer: SHAP attributions expose per-feature contributions, while Attention Fusion
Network (AFN) attention weights reveal which specialist domain drove each decision, pro-

Problem Statement
Financial fraud is rapidly escalating alongside the growth of real-time digital transactions.
Existing systems analyze transactions in isolation, missing the behavioural and relational
context that fraud rings exploit. Legacy fraud detection systems, which rely on rigid rules
and opaque models, are failing to keep pace with evolving threats, resulting in severe secu-
rity gaps and significant financial losses.
The core challenges are fourfold: data imbalance—with fraud comprising only approxi-
mately 3.5% of transactions—prevents reliable minority-class model training; single-model
architectures lack the specialist depth required to simultaneously capture transaction, be-
havioural, and graph-level fraud signals; systems operate as “black boxes” lacking the audit

architectures lack the specialist depth required to simultaneously capture transaction, be-
havioural, and graph-level fraud signals; systems operate as “black boxes” lacking the audit
transparency demanded by regulators; and they are too slow or architecturally inflexible to
meet the critical sub-5-second real-time latency requirement. Furthermore, limited access
to true behavioural and labeled fraud data, and the difficulty of detecting authorized-but-
malicious transactions, represent additional open challenges. An integrated, hybrid solution
that combines predictive speed, specialist depth, and Explainable AI is therefore required to
unify detection across all channels, minimize costly False Positives, and ensure continuous,
adaptive defense against evolving fraud tactics.

3.3
Methodology
3.3.1
Data Ingestion and Preprocessing
The methodology begins with the IEEE CIS Fraud Detection dataset of 590,000 transac-
tions, comprising transaction and identity Comma-Separated Values (CSV) files left-joined
on TransactionID to preserve all rows. Categorical features are label-encoded with NaN
values mapped to −1 consistently across training, streaming, and API entry points. Four
globally-leaky columns are explicitly identified as a shared constant and dropped at every
stage—training, Kafka producer, and inference—to prevent data leakage. Class imbalance
(approximately 3.5% fraud rate) is handled via Weighted Random Sampler (WeRS) over-
sampling in the DataLoader and AsymmetricLoss (Focal + Tversky) during training. The
specialist models (transaction, behavioural, graph) also optionally use a 100-tree LightGBM

sampling in the DataLoader and AsymmetricLoss (Focal + Tversky) during training. The
specialist models (transaction, behavioural, graph) also optionally use a 100-tree LightGBM
warm-start whose probability output is appended as an additional input feature, giving the
neural network a strong residual signal to refine. The number of LightGBM estimators was
reduced from 200–300 to 100 per specialist to halve warm-start training time on CPU while
preserving signal quality.
In the live pipeline, transaction and identity records are Kafka-streamed and enriched via a
Redis–PostgreSQL feature store. The producer streams data in 5,000-row chunks (with the
first batch available in approximately 2 seconds); the consumer batches records to 1,024 per
inference call, performing a single-round-trip Redis fetch to retrieve precomputed features.
A serialized schema enforces strict training-inference parity, with missing features defaulted
to 0 at inference time.

3.3.2
Feature Engineering
Six domain-specific modules extract features independently: static card and account at-
tributes, identity and device signals, temporal patterns, graph-based structural features, cross-
entity aggregations, and product-level signals. All historical aggregate features are com-
puted using cumulative-shift rolling statistics—shift-before-rolling—to guarantee zero look-
ahead bias and reflect real-world inference conditions. Key derived signals include veloc-
ity windows, transaction amount z-scores, and graph degree centrality metrics that surface
shared-infrastructure fraud rings by counting unique devices per card and unique cards per
device.

CHAPTER 4
RESULTS AND DISCUSSION
The system was evaluated across three iterative trials, progressively eliminating data leakage
and architectural deficiencies.
Trial 1 (Exploration) evaluated nine individual algorithms—including Random Forest,
XGBoost, Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), and Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP)—on the
credit card and Unified Payments Interface (UPI) datasets. SMOTE was applied before the
train-test split, introducing data leakage, and the LSTM used a timestep of 1, negating its
sequence modeling capability.
Trial 2 (Redesign) introduced three specialist models for transaction, behavioural, and
graph risk signals, augmented by a FraudFormer Transformer and a Reinforcement Learn-
ing (RL) agent for cost-aware decisions. However, the behavioural and graph models were
retrained on the full dataset, and the RL agent was trained on overfitted specialist scores,
invalidating evaluation metrics.

invalidating evaluation metrics.

of-Fold (OOF) scoring eliminates score leakage for the transaction specialist; shift-based

5.1
Conclusion
The Real-Time Financial Fraud Detection System successfully evolved across three itera-
tive trials into a mature, three-specialist ensemble orchestrated by an AFN, delivering an
adaptive, transparent, and high-speed defense against modern financial fraud. A fourth ar-
chitectural layer, the UFNN, consolidates the specialist stack into a single end-to-end CPU-
optimised model using group-level feature encoders and cross-group multi-head attention,
providing a maintainable and deployment-ready alternative to the prior multi-file agentic
architecture.
The project thoroughly analyzed the requirements of bank-grade fraud detection—sub-5-
second latency, regulatory transparency, and continuous adaptability—and finalized an ap-
propriate technology stack comprising PyTorch for both the AFN and the UFNN, gradient-
boosted tree ensembles as optional warm-start specialists, Apache Kafka for real-time stream-

propriate technology stack comprising PyTorch for both the AFN and the UFNN, gradient-
boosted tree ensembles as optional warm-start specialists, Apache Kafka for real-time stream-
ing with stateless per-transaction scoring and dead-letter queue error handling, Redis and
PostgreSQL for the feature store, FastAPI for serving, and Next.js for the dashboard. CPU
optimisations — MKL-DNN kernel acceleration, torch.inference_mode(), vectorised graph
feature construction, and torch.compile — are applied consistently across all model files.
The core predictive foundation was established with rigorous attention to data hygiene. The
critical lesson of data leakage was addressed by applying OOF scoring to the transaction
specialist and using shift-based rolling aggregates across all feature modules, eliminating
look-ahead bias. The Attention Meta Learner achieved a precision of 0.8445 and ROC-
AUC of 0.9710, while the Meta Risk Model reached a ROC-AUC of 0.9721, establishing

a high-reliability predictive layer. The multi-tier decision routing—with its 11-rule cascade
auto-resolving the review zone—keeps human intervention below 3%, meeting the 97%+

The system’s explainability layer, combining SHAP TreeExplainer attributions with AFN
